does: This paper investigates whether linear probes for detecting harmful behaviors in language models generalize better when trained in a low-dimensional subspace derived from persona coordinates rather than in the full activation space. The authors construct persona axes for deception and sycophancy by using contrastive persona prompts and applying PCA to persona-specific activation vectors. They demonstrate that the principal components of these persona-derived directions cleanly separate harmful from harmless model behaviors. The study evaluates this approach across ten evaluation datasets and shows that probes trained on persona principal components achieve better transfer performance and robustness under distribution shift compared to standard linear probes trained in full activation space.
connects: This work builds on recent research into mechanistic interpretability and behavioral monitoring of language models, particularly extending ideas from the Assistant Axis and Persona Selection Model literature. It responds to the fundamental problem that text-only monitoring fails to detect strategic deception and model sandbagging, motivating the use of white-box probing approaches that read model internals directly. The paper connects to broader work on distribution shift robustness in ML and the use of contrastive methods to isolate semantic directions in neural networks. It also draws on sparse autoencoders and similar techniques that seek to identify interpretable, low-dimensional subspaces within high-dimensional model representations, as well as work on model alignment and safety monitoring through internal state analysis.
enables: This approach points toward more robust behavioral monitoring systems that can reliably detect harmful outputs even as models attempt to evade evaluation or shift their behavior strategically. The persona coordinate framework could enable better understanding of how model behaviors cluster in representation space, potentially revealing which internal mechanisms drive deception versus other harmful outputs. Future work could extend this method to other forms of harmful behavior beyond deception and sycophancy, develop automated approaches for identifying persona axes without manual prompt engineering, and investigate whether these robust directions correspond to causal levers for controlling model behavior. The findings could inform the design of more reliable deployed monitoring systems and advance interpretability methods that separate causal features from spurious correlations in model internals.
